raw:
[PAPER COMPLETO — 29887 caracteres, 6 páginas]

Emotional Tissue: A Visual Representation of
Characters’ Emotional States in Games
Jonas de Ara´ujo Luz Junior
Tribunal Regional Eleitoral do Cear´a
TRE-CE
Fortaleza-CE, Brazil
jonas@tre-ce.jus.br
Maria Andr´eia Formico Rodrigues
Programa de P´os-Graduac¸˜ao em Inform´atica Aplicada (PPGIA)
Universidade de Fortaleza (UNIFOR)
Fortaleza-CE, Brazil
mafr@unifor.br
Abstract—In empathy games, the representation of the char-
acter’s emotional states during the narrative is relevant. For this
purpose, the designer of empathy games uses different resources,
such as the game’s graphical style, sound and visual effects,
soundtrack mood, and narrative style and pace, among others.
Although these can effectively transmit emotion, they will still
be subject to the player’s interpretive subjectivity. However,
there are situations where the designer wants the player to
understand the character’s emotional state, especially when it is
the focus of gameplay and fundamental to the player’s decisions.
Unlike conventional level bars and their visual variations to
represent characters’ emotional states, this work presents a new
approach for defining a widget to express emotional states,
modelled as an organic structure in the form of a shaded
cloth deformation. We directly relate its pulse frequency to
the intensity of the characters’ emotions and its colours to the
characters’ feelings defined by the game designer. In addition, we
conducted a preliminary performance comparative study with the
widget configured with four, six and eight emotions and created
demonstration scenes to validate its use.
Index Terms—emotion visualization, empathy games, UX, GUI
widget
I. INTRODUCTION
The representation of emotional states in graphical user in-
terfaces is generally a subject neglected [1], mainly due to the
complexity of its capture and representation [2]. Nevertheless,
visual communication of emotions and emotional states are
useful resources that one can use, for example, to inform
the emotional state of the computer users, favouring self-
awareness and, in the case of collaborative systems, support
awareness [1], [2]. Moreover, in empathy games [3], [4], the
player must recognize the emotion the character expresses
to make it effective. To this end, the graphics, soundtrack,
special effects, narrative, etc., all act together to communicate
emotions to the audience. In particular, the techniques used
in digital games do not differ too much from those used in
other media and have been inherited from cinematography.
However, in the case of digital games, there is a crucial game
design cornerstone that does not exist in other media, such as
in the cinema and literature, which is the gameplay.
When emotion recognition is essential in the context of
gameplay, it is necessary to represent it clearly in the game’s
user interface to convey the desired sequence of the characters’
978-1-6654-6156-6/22/$31.00 ©2022 IEEE
Fig. 1. Image composition of the Emotional Tissue widget configured with
four, six and eight emotions.
emotions or emotional states. While we can do this using
conventional widgets such as level bars, life bars or expe-
rience (XP) bars, and their variations, as game designers and
developers, we believe that other visual forms of representing
emotions could be explored. Therefore, we foresee that there
are still issues to address and challenges to manage and
overcome in this research area. Moreover, we understand that
conventional widgets have a visual aspect and interpretation
too mechanical to represent something as organic as emotions.
Thus, we seek a way to describe the emotional state of
characters more organically and vividly.
This paper presents Emotional Tissue, a widget we designed
to inform characters’ emotional states in digital games, con-
figured with four, six and eight emotions (Fig. 1). Our general
goal is to favour the use of characters’ emotional states as
gameplay elements, especially in empathy games, where the
communication of emotions is crucial along with the narrative.
II. BACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK
Cernea et al. [5] while recognizing the advantages of
standardizing the desktop environment graphical user inter-
face components —like a pointer, windows, input fields and
buttons—, also point out its disadvantages. These authors em-
phasize that, such standardization communicates objective in-
formation and navigation commands in the interaction process
between user and machine, but combining these standardized
components with greater flexibility is difficult. As a possible
solution, they propose Emotion Scents [5], as a guideline
for representing user emotions in standard UI widgets. The
emotion scent representation is based on associating a seg-


mented colour bar with standard GUI components; the length
of the bar is predefined and the colour segments range from
red (representing negative emotions) to green (representing
positive emotions). A segmented bar of blue colours represents
the user experience of variation of arousal when interacting
with the GUI, indicating the excitement levels.
The research by Cernea et al. evolves into patterns of
visualization of user emotion in multi-touch interfaces, which
they called Emotion Prints [2], [6]. Instead of the bar format
of the emotion scent, the emotion print has a circular format,
favouring its positioning in the multi-touch interface, at the
exact point of the user’s touch. Emotion Prints also use
colours. However, they range from blue tones to red. The
colour is associated with the pulse intensity of the emotion
print, representing the arousal intensity of the emotion. The
emotion valence, whether negative or positive, is represented
by the outline texture of emotion print [2].
Kovaˇcevi´c et al. present a two glyph-based widget types [1].
One indicates the current affective state, and the other shows
the current trend of the affective state over time, that is, the
mood. The authors chose the circular format for their widgets
because it proved suitable for different screen orientations and
simultaneously unobtrusive. In addition to the two emotional
dimensions —valence and arousal— represented by Cernea
et al. Emotion Scents and Emotion Prints, the glyph-based
widgets from Kovaˇcevi´c et al. also describe a third emotional
dimension, i.e., dominance, incorporated by adapting the ra-
dius of the widgets’ base circle [1].
Cernea et al. focus on representing the emotional states of
computer users, including making real-time measurements of
EEG brain signals through headset [2] [5]. Kovaˇcevi´c et al.
model instantaneous emotional states (emotions), as well as
over time (mood), considering three dimensions of emotion:
valence, arousal and dominance [1].
The widget we present in this work is similar to the
previously mentioned works in some specific aspects, such
as the circular format (although represented in perspective),
use of colours, and “pulse”, the latter also helpful to in-
dicate the arousal of the emotion. However, there are also
distinct differences between Emotional Tissue and existing
works. More specifically, its visual appearance focuses on
an animated organic-like shaded shape. In addition, in those
related works, the widget’s colours (one of the widgets’ con-
struction elements) are predefined. In our widget, the colours
are parameterizable, favouring their adaptation by the game
developer to the colour palette or general mood of the target
game to be developed. Another significant difference in our
work is its focus on the context of digital games, especially
empathy games, which use characters’ emotional states as
essential gameplay elements. In contrast, previously cited
works focus on the user’s emotional representation. Moreover,
we also implemented an API considering game designers as
potential users of our widget solution. With this objective, the
availability of an API for the game designer or game developer
is critical.
III. DESIGN GOALS
Our design goal is to express characters’ emotional states
through an organic visual representation to help game de-
signers to convey characters’ emotions during cinematics and
gameplay. For this purpose, we split up our design goals into
smaller goals as follows:
• Goal 1: Provide a visualization tool of a character’s
internal emotional state in video games, which we can
incorporate into the UI of the game or application as a
widget,
• Goal 2: Allow content game designers to quickly gen-
erate characters’ emotional states through a simple and
flexible widget modification API, and
• Goal 3: Present use cases to show the features of the
widget we developed with scenes that illustrate different
characters and their respective emotional states.
IV. WIDGET DESCRIPTION
Emotional Tissue is a visual composite of a cloth defor-
mation filled with a material that uses a specifically designed
shader. In addition, we created an animation as a pulsating
motion to represent the inner emotional state of each game
character. We represent a specific emotion (or emotions) by the
predominant colour emitted by the shader, while the intensity
of the colour and the pulse indicate the emotion’s arousal level.
The inspiration for Emotional Tissue came from observing
and studying relevant design approaches, such as those pro-
posed by Hughes [7], Cernea et al. [2], [5], and Kovaˇcevi´c et
al. [1]. Like Emotion Prints from Cernea et al. [2] and the
widgets by Kovaˇcevi´c et al. [1], Emotional Tissue is based on
a circular format. However, we opted to represent our widget
in a 3D perspective view to enhance the visual representation
of its dynamic shape and pulse to the game designer.
Fig. 1 presents the Emotional Tissue configured with four,
six and eight emotions and their respective assigned colours.
The widget colours and the feelings associated with them are
abstractions defined by the game designer. In two demonstra-
tion scenes, we used an instance of Emotional Tissue in which
two different characters simulate four emotions: Joy, Sadness,
Love and Hate (or Anger). We will discuss this in more detail
in Section VIII.
V. IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS
We created the current implementation of the widget in the
Unity Engine (version 2021.3.x), using Shader Graph v. 12.1.7
[8] and the Cloth component [9]. Regarding programming, we
used C#, the game engine’s default language.
The mesh we use in Emotional Tissue corresponds to a
plane on which we apply the Cloth component [9] and the
developed shader. With six sphere colliders, we can control
the movement of the used cloth. We designed and applied
to these colliders another component, named Hammer. The
frequency and amplitude of the hammers’ vibrations are
controlled by an additional component we implemented, the
HammerManager.


Fig. 2. Emotional Tissue core architecture diagram.
We coloured the geometric mesh with our specific shader —
which we named EmotionalShader— implemented using the
official Unity extension Shader Graph [8]. EmotionalShader
has as input parameters the colours and intensities of the
emotions abstracted by it. The colours are evenly distributed
in a circle and mixed with a Voronoi procedural pattern [10]
to create a more organic visual reminiscent of cellular tissue.
The vertical positions of the mesh vertices change according
to the frequency of their animations. In addition, we use them
to determine the degree of light emission and metallic tone of
the material, thus amplifying the pulsating effect, colours, and
brightness of the mesh surface.
The current shader version we present supports four, six and
eight basic emotions/colours. Furthermore, the possibility of
the shader receiving an arbitrary number of emotions/colours
is being investigated by using a texture as a colour map
associated with a parameter that indicates the number of cells
existing in the map.
We
developed
two
components
to
control
and
in-
tegrate
the
functioning
of
the
Emotional
Tissue
mod-
ules: (1) the ClothController, which directly controls
the Cloth component applied to the mesh, and (2) the
EmotionalTissueControl, which implements the gen-
eral widget controller, aiming to be used as a UI control. The
relationship between these components is shown in Fig. 2,
displaying the core architecture of our Emotional Tissue.
To be used as a widget or UI control, we organize the
Emotional Tissue as a Unity prefab. Integrating the prefab,
there are: the EmotionalTissue GameObject and associ-
ated component’s class which implements Emotional Tissue’s
core logic, the GameObject that represents the Cloth, the
six sphere colliders (hammers) and their HammerManager
GameObject. In addition, the prefab comprises a camera
and a UI Canvas, which implement the real-time conversion
of the 3D view of EmotionalTissue into a two-dimensional
texture, presented as a UI control or widget. The technique
used to convert the 3D view of the EmotionalTissue
GameObject into 2D for use in the UI is well known. It has
been traditionally applied to creating mini-maps in the Unity
Engine: the camera that makes up the prefab captures the 3D
view and renders it to a Unity RenderTexture, which is, in turn,
displayed in the prefab’s Canvas in a RawImage component.
VI. WIDGET FEATURES AND USE POSSIBILITIES
By allowing the player to objectively recognise the game
characters’ emotional states, the designer of an empathy game
can use this resource as a gameplay mechanism. For example,
to plan actions and events that, according to the player’s
agency, one is free to reverse, improve, stimulate, or maintain
the emotional state manifested by the character.
To make use of our new widget approach, the game designer
will represent and update the characters’ emotional states
through the prefab and API that integrate the Emotional Tissue
package, detailed as follows:
1) Place the prefab in the scene under development,
2) Adjust the position and size of the widget through the
Panel component available within the Canvas existing
in the prefab as desired. Optionally, a more experienced
designer can unpack the prefab and customize the Can-
vas elements or even integrate them into another Canvas,
if one exists and the designer prefers it, and
3) Program the scripts that will define the setup and the
logic for using the widget through the API provided.
The logic of using Emotional Tissue is entirely up to
the game designer and depends on the role emotions play
in gameplay. Although there are no impediments to using
EmotionalShader directly, the prefab offers an API that
allows changing the emotions’ colours and their corresponding
intensities. To make use of the API, the programmer must
initially retrieve the EmotionTissueControl component,
which offers the following methods:
• void InitColors(NumberOfColors noc) - Initialize the
widget with the selected number of colours: 4, 6 or 8.
• void Add(string emotion, Color color)
-
Sets
an
emotion and its colour. Used to setup Emotional Tissue.
• Color GetColor(int index) - Retrieves the colour of
the shader parameter of the given index.
• Color GetColor(string emotion) - Retrieves the colour
assigned to the target emotion.
• void SetColor(int index, Color color) - Assigns the
indicated colour to the colour parameter with the given
index.
• void SetColor(string emotion, Color color) - As-
signs the specified colour to an emotion.
• float GetValue(int index) - Retrieves the intensity
value, normalized between 0 and 1, of the colour with
the given index parameter.
• float GetValue(string emotion) - Retrieves the in-
tensity value, normalized between 0 and 1, of a given
emotion.
• void SetValue(int index, float value) - Assigns the
intensity value, normalized between 0 and 1, of the colour
parameter with the given index.


• void SetValue(string emotion, float value) - Sets
the intensity value, normalized between 0 and 1, of the
given emotion.
• void ResetValue(int index) - Resets the colour inten-
sity value with parameter with the given index.
• ResetValue(string emotion) - Resets the intensity
value of a given emotion.
Figure 3 gives an example of using the API, for four distinct
emotions. After the code execution, the resulting Emotional
Tissue widget is shown on the right.
Fig. 3. A code example of Emotional Tissue API calls and the corresponding
visual result.
VII. A PRELIMINARY PERFORMANCE COMPARISON
We used the Unity Profiler and the new Unity Profile
Analyzer [11] in their default settings to make a prelimi-
nary performance comparative study of the Emotional Tis-
sue through the Camera.Render, ClothController.Update() and
UnityEngine::Cloth.getVertices() markers, measured in mil-
liseconds. We conducted the tests, with four, six and eight
emotions, on a computer with an AMD Ryzen 9 5950X 16-
Core Processor, 64GB of RAM and an NVIDIA GeForce
RTX 3080 GPU. Each test was performed four times, and
the average of each median value was used to generate the
results shown in Table I.
TABLE I

The Camera.Render marker allows to indirectly evaluate
the performance of the implemented shader, while the other
markers aim to evaluate the cloth simulation performance. The
preliminary results show that, as the number of emotions in-
creases, there is a slight increase in the execution time of each
frame—but the execution time variation of the Camera.Render
marker is negligible, remaining around 0.20-0.22ms, and so
is the variation of the other markers. However, we should
analyse these values with caution, especially given the widget’s
aim to function as a GUI element, which should not be
too expensive for the hardware. In future work, setting more
complex benchmarks and conducting new performance tests
will be necessary to identify our widget’s main advantages
and limitations, helping to revise and improve it.
VIII. DEMONSTRATION SCENES
We created two demonstration scenes with two different
characters to validate the use of the widget, as shown in
Fig. 4. These scenes are also simple examples of how the
game designer could use Emotional Tissue. Each character
expresses four emotions: Joy, Sadness, Love and Hate (or
Anger). The game designer defined these emotions and their
respective colours during development, but the player sets the
emotions’ intensities through sliders at runtime. For the screen-
shots shown in Fig. 4, we have assigned each emotion at its
maximum intensity for each fine-tuned character’s emotions.
(a) Joy.
(b) Sadness.
(c) Love.
(d) Anger/Hate.
Fig. 4.
Screenshots of the demonstration scenes, with use case examples
of Emotional Tissue, set with four distinct emotions tuned distinctly for two
different characters.
Each demonstration scene has a second UI Canvas, which
maintains a panel with sliders that determine the emotional


Fig. 5. Demonstration scene EmotionsSetup component.
states of the 3D characters. The manipulation of the sliders
determines the predominant emotion, reflected in the char-
acters’ facial expressions and the customized widgets of our
Emotional Tissue, shown on the top right of each screenshot
of Fig. 4. The 3D characters used were licensed from Daz 3D
[12], which already has the facial blendshapes associated with
the emotions used in the demonstration experiment. However,
in future versions, we plan to replace these characters with
others freely accessible to allow their distribution together with
Emotional Tissue.
The content of the sliders panel is populated during
scene loading from a setup prepared at design time and
maintained in a component created for this, which we call
EmotionsSetup, shown in Fig. 5. For each character, the
EmotionsSetup component stores their list of emotions,
the associated colours and blendshapes, and the pairs of
opposing emotions, which appear in the same slider in the
scene interface. The appearance of the fields drawer that
identifies the emotion in the Unity inspector, its colour and
associated blend shapes is customized through Unity’s UI
Toolkit [13], through the EmotionSetting class and as-
sociated eponymous UXML file. In the example shown in
Fig. 5 we define the four emotions used in the scenes and
their related parameters.
At runtime, this setup is read by the DialsPanel com-
ponent and applied to the UI panel in the scene’s Canvas.
First, for each pair of emotions, DialsPanel instantiates
a GameObject based on a prefab named Emotion_Panel,
which contains the slider and two labels indicating the
emotions of the pair. Then, it initializes its values and the
callback function. The content and bindings of each in-
stance of Emotion_Panel is internally controlled by a
EmotionPanel component. Fig. 6 shows the scene code’s
architecture and the relationships between these elements’ C#
classes. The DialsPanel component acts, in fact, as the
Fig. 6. An example of scene’s code architecture.
scene controller, and it is also responsible for programming the
response to the change in the value of each slider. The callback
function that DialsPanel assigns to each EmotionPanel
allows it to respond to the change in the value of the sliders,
identifying the predominant emotion and its intensity. From
this, DialsPanel triggers the intensity variation in the blend
shapes associated with that specific emotional state (expressed
by the character) and in the widget of the Emotional Tissue,
which is the objective of this demonstration. For example,
Fig. 4 shows the maximum variations of each emotion tuned
distinctly for the characters.
Finally, the approach presented in these demonstration
scenes is just one of several possibilities, focused on varying
a character’s emotional state and its reflection in Emotional
Tissue through sliders. Through the API, and according to
the game designer’s goal and intention, the simplicity and
flexibility of Emotional Tissue may allow its use in other
contexts, for example, in narratives where the player’s choices
have a consequence on the characters’ emotional states, or in
cases where an AI mechanism define the emotional states,
among others. Nevertheless, in addition to validating the
practical use of Emotional Tissue, these demonstration scenes
serve as references to guide its use.
IX. GOAL-ORIENTED RESULTS
Regarding Goal 1, we developed a visualization tool that
can be incorporated into the UI of a Unity game or application,
representing characters’ emotional states through a 3D struc-
ture with an organic, colourful and pulsating appearance. The
structure’s colours, brightness and metallic appearance change
according to colour and intensity parameters. Although we ini-
tially designed and developed this solution to be used in a UI,


its structure naturally allows its incorporation directly into a
3D scenario. In addition, it enables the game designer to adapt
the semantics of its colours and intensities as needed. One of
the advantages of our approach resides in the visualization
tool, which allows the association between the original idea
of associating cloth and shading in a single coherent structure
while keeping it simple and flexible. In addition, the Goal 2
was also fully met, essentially through the implementation of
the EmotionalTissueControl component, which fulfils
the role of the controller, working as the focal point of
the Emotional Tissue’s API calls. Finally, the demonstration
scenes presented in this work also satisfactorily achieves the
Goal 3, additionally serving as a programming tutorial for the
logic needed to act on Emotional Tissue.
X. CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK
In addition to satisfying the three design goals of this
work, the prototype version of Emotional Tissue proved to be
practical and flexible, and may be potentially valuable for nar-
ratives where the player’s choices have a consequence on the
characters’ emotional states or in cases where AI mechanisms
define the emotional states. Our preliminary performance study
using widgets with different numbers of emotions/colours (i.e.,
four, six and eight) showed similar results for the markers
related to the shader and the cloth simulation. Thus, a widget
with eight emotions/colours would offer greater flexibility to
the game designer without sacrificing overall performance.
However, setting more complex benchmarks and new perfor-
mance tests will aid to identify our widget’s main limitations,
helping to revise and improve it. We also successfully created
two demonstration scenes to validate the use of the widget,
showing how the game designers can use Emotional Tissue,
by defining emotions and their colours, with the player setting
the emotions’ intensities through sliders at runtime.
In future work, usability and UX testings (ideally with
designers of empathy games) will help to access and validate
the ability of the proposed widget to convey the character’s
emotions objective and unambiguously. In addition, we plan
to implement code optimizations and performance analyses
as the Emotional Tissue develops, including low-level imple-
mentation of the widget’s core. These studies will indicate its
positive/negative points, eventually leading to its adaptation to
a simpler 2D shape, still preserving the main features of the 3D
mesh but with no extra computational costs. Another possible
extension is the generation of an EmotionalShader with
a limit of 16 or possibly 32 colours. Systematic tests and
profiling of the EmotionalShader on computers, mobile
devices, TVs and web platforms will be mandatory to verify
what this colour limit will be.
Also, the polygonal mesh used in the current implementa-
tion is a shaded circular 3D cutting plane. This choice was
intuitive to represent the idealized organic look, the most
common in tests and applications of the Cloth component.
However, the EmotionalShader and its associated material
can be applied to any mesh. Thus, we also aim to experiment
with custom meshes to offer the game designer the flexibil-
ity to adapt the widget to the game theme. Moreover, the
EmotionalShader was implemented and tested in Unity’s
native rendering engine, and must be validated in the exist-
ing scriptable render pipelines: the universal render pipeline
(URP), and the high-definition render pipeline (HDRP) [14].
The idea of extending the visual effects applied to the shader
in these sophisticated rendering engines is not ruled out, but
should be carefully evaluated, so as not to negatively impact
the simplicity and uniformity of Emotional Tissue. Finally, we
aim to publish future versions of Emotional Tissue as open-
source, under the MIT license [15] and, eventually, as a free
product in the Unity Asset Store [16]. We also consider the
possibility of porting Emotional Tissue to other game engines,
notably the Unreal Engine and Godot Engine.
ACKNOWLEDGMENT
Andr´eia Formico would like to thank the University of For-
taleza (UNIFOR) for its financial support (Ed. DPDI 79/2020).
